replies.author: @BryanMontford
replies.text: One of the very first thoughts I had about graphene was cabling. Specifically, speaker cables, headphone leads, and replacement wiring in Tube mics for studio use. The very second thought was, "Oh yah, this is capacitive..." My poorly educated take on it is that you'd have to insure that it would pass frequencies from 20Hz to potentially 24KHz. A single film capacitor is after all the simplest way to limit low frequency information providing a tapering cutoff. I have no idea what the capacitive properties of graphene are, or how one might go about tailoring those properties. Also, last I chatted with Mr. (Dr.?) Murray-Smith about coating materials with graphene, it seemed well past anything within the range of a hobbyist, at least as far as being able to retain any of the beneficial properties of the graphene. I was specifically discussing electroplating type deposition onto alloys or an infusion into the dye faze of Anodization to impregnate the oxide layer with graphene. His conclusion at the time was that it wouldn't be very effective applied via that method. (By "hobbiest", I mean someone with a reasonably well stocked machine shop and abilities to fabricate via standard subtract and additive methods, and including electro or chemically reactive surface treatments, painting, printing, molding, casting, vacuum forming, vector cutting materials and such.)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Bryan Montford
Hiya mate, it's Rob incidentally - the simplest way of tailoring graphene properties is by monoatomic doping- the addition of function groups can be done to graphene oxide because of the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl and epoxy sites introduced by the oxidation of graphite - this is one of the reasons why graphene oxide is good to add to some polymers followed by in situ reduction strategies. There ae hobbiest and hobbiest - by which i mean some people have a table and kitchen sink and some have a pretty well equipped lab - which includes several friends of mine - so, though beyond the table and sink guys it is not betond the more determined hobbiest lol

replies.author: @BryanMontford
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Epoxies, resins, polymers - now we're speaking terms I not only comprehend, but have some proficiency with. Some mixing cups, something to fabricate a form and some non-sulfur clay are about all you need to jump into the polymer-elastomer pool. With supervision, it's a third to fourth grade level activity that you can chase until you're old enough to make your own resin casket... I still can't manage to wrap my brain around why Graphene has not already been applied in so many different capacities commercially. There hardly seems many areas you can't apply Graphene in some means to make it more efficient, effective, and often times less expensive.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Bryan Montford
lol - there are probably a few reasons and i would guess the two main ones are it's not that easy to make and business has a natural conservatism - with a small c

author: @FredGandt
text:
As I watched the first antenna video, I thought of NFC (Near Field Communication) enabled devices and how important it is for their antenna's to be matched with the tags they read. This would be idea for  #Grinders  and their like who are building devices in their garages and bedrooms :-)

I wonder (I freely admit a lack of knowledge) if tuning can be done with layers and/or shielding to boost and/or suppress the signal thingy, so the shape of the design becomes essentially irrelevant? Erm ... I mean, the butterfly design is beautifully simple and works the best. It seems that a lot of stuff is over-engineered, but sometimes fine tuning requires a lot of fiddly extra stuff (I'm sleepy, sorry).
Maybe the painted application could allow for fine tuning by simple painting some bits thicker than other bits?

I'm going to bed :-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i think you are right mate and nfc is a really good idea

replies.author: @FredGandt
replies.text:
Cool :-)
Paging ***** and Lucas Dummitt and tagging  #Transhumanism  and  #Implants  and then going to sleep.
